topic: Cline AI Review 2026: Open-Source VS Code Coding Agent Tested

competitors.title: Cline AI Review 2026: Open-Source AI Coding Agent Tested
key_points: Cline is an open-source autonomous coding agent for VS Code with 5M+ installs, 58K GitHub stars
BYOK (Bring Your Own Key) advantage: connects to any AI provider via API key (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Mistral, DeepSeek, local models via Ollama)
Autonomous agent capabilities: reads/writes files, executes terminal commands, launches headless browser for testing, supports MCP (Model Context Protocol)
VS Code extension, not a fork: installs into existing VS Code vs Cursor which forks VS Code
Model flexibility: switch models mid-conversation based on task, budget, preference
Cost transparency: shows token counts, cost per request, cumulative spend in real-time
No inline completions: agent vs autocomplete tool, needs explicit task requests
Setup complexity: requires API keys configuration vs Cursor/Copilot out-of-box experience
Variable costs with heavy Opus usage can run $10-20/day in API credits

competitors.title: Cline Review (2026): Pricing, Features, and Alternatives
key_points: VS Code-first coding agent for multi-step task execution with approval gates
Core strength: multi-file changes plus command execution vs single-snippet assistants
Human-in-the-loop approvals: every file change and shell command requires approval, main safety layer
Terminal and browser capabilities: runs commands to install packages, run builds, inspect errors; automates browser checks for UI flows
MCP extensibility: wire in extra tools and internal systems
Rating: 8.6/10 - stronger for actual execution vs suggestions
Free tier: extension free, cost is model usage through provider API keys
VS Code users who want agent execution without changing IDE vs terminal-native approaches

competitors.title: Cline vs Roo Code vs Continue (2026) | DevToolReviews
key_points: Three VS Code AI extension leaders: Cline (autonomous agents), Roo Code (workflow control), Continue (contextual help)
Cline is original agentic powerhouse: reads files, creates files, runs build, fixes errors until task complete
Uses Model Context Protocol (MCP) to connect to external tools like web browser, database client, documentation indexer
Complex task can cost $0.50-$1.00 in API credits due to frequent 'thinking' and 'looping'
Agentic loops: excellent but expensive with Claude 3.5 Sonnet tokens
Performance benchmarks: chat latency 420ms, time to first edit (refactor) 2.1s
Comparison: Cline for junior developer automation, Roo Code for safety/modes, Continue for codebase context/local models
Best model for agentic work: Claude 3.5 Sonnet gold standard in 2026

stats.fact: Cline has 5M+ VS Code extension installs and 58,000+ GitHub stars (BuildFastWithAI 2026)
Complex multi-step tasks using Cline with Claude 3.5 Sonnet cost $0.50-$1.00 in API credits per task (DevToolReviews 2026)
Cline chat latency: 420ms first token, time to first edit (refactor) 2.1s (DevToolReviews benchmarks)
Cline rating: 8.6/10 for multi-step execution with approval gates vs 8.5 for Continue and 8.6 for Roo Code (DevToolReviews)
Heavy Opus 4.6 usage with Cline can run $10-20/day in API credits (BuildFastWithAI 2026)
Cline supports any OpenAI-compatible API including local models via Ollama for zero API cost (VibeCoding)
75% of developers using AI tools reach for code generation, 60% turn to debugging tools (ZipDo AI Statistics 2026)

angles: Open-source vs subscription: BYOK advantage with model flexibility vs vendor lock-in
VS Code extension vs fork: staying in official VS Code vs migrating to Cursor/Windsurf forks
Autonomous agent vs autocomplete: multi-step task execution vs inline suggestions
Cost transparency: real-time token tracking vs subscription pricing surprise bills
Human-in-the-loop safety: approval gates for production safety vs fully autonomous agents
MCP extensibility: connecting external tools vs closed assistant experiences
Developer workflow optimization: Cline for heavy tasks + Continue for daily driving combination
outline: Introduction: Open-source VS Code AI coding agent revolution in 2026
What is Cline and how it works: BYOK model, autonomous agent capabilities
Key features: Multi-step task execution, terminal/browser automation, MCP extensibility
Pricing and costs: Free extension + API credits, real-world cost patterns
Strengths: Model flexibility, cost transparency, human-in-the-loop approvals
Weaknesses: No inline completions, setup complexity, variable costs
Cline vs Cursor: VS Code extension vs fork, agent vs autocomplete
Cline vs Claude Code: IDE vs terminal agent paradigms
Cline vs Roo Code vs Continue: Feature comparison matrix
Performance benchmarks: Latency, time to edit, memory usage
Best use cases: When Cline excels vs when alternatives are better
Setup guide: Installation, API configuration, model selection
Workflow optimization: Combining Cline with other tools
Future outlook: Cline's place in 2026-2027 AI coding landscape
Expert recommendations: Which developers should choose Cline
Conclusion: The open-source agentic advantage for VS Code loyalists
